description: Cross-skill integration scenario: instruction + prompt + hook working together to enforce a team workflow

scenario.title: Enforce Conventional Commits with an instruction, a prompt, and a hook
scenario.setup: A team wants Claude to: (1) always follow Conventional Commits format, (2) generate commit messages using a structured prompt, and (3) automatically validate the message before any git commit.

steps.user_request: Write workspace instructions so Claude always uses Conventional Commits format when writing commit messages

key_rules: All commit messages must follow Conventional Commits: <type>(<scope>): <subject>
Valid types: feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, test, chore
Subject line max 72 characters; no period at end
Breaking changes must include 'BREAKING CHANGE:' in the footer

steps.user_request: Create a zero-shot prompt that generates a Conventional Commits message from a diff

key_elements: Output format constraint: exactly one Conventional Commits line
Input: git diff content passed as {{DIFF}}
Grounding instruction: base message only on provided diff
No explanatory text — commit message only

steps.user_request: Add a PreToolUse hook that validates any git commit command uses Conventional Commits format

key_behaviors: Returns decision: block with reason if invalid

integration_assertions: Instruction file does not conflict with prompt template (both enforce same format)
Hook validation regex matches the format spec in the instruction file
Prompt output passes the hook validator when given a clean diff
All three artifacts can be deployed together without contradiction
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Instruction says 'use Conventional Commits' but prompt generates free-form messages
Hook regex is stricter than the instruction spec (false positives)
Prompt includes explanatory text that gets committed as-is (hook blocks it)
